patterns.name: API Gateway
patterns.description: Single entry point for all client requests, handles routing, auth, rate limiting
patterns.when_to_use: When exposing multiple microservices to external clients

patterns.name: Circuit Breaker
patterns.description: Prevents cascading failures by stopping calls to failing services
patterns.when_to_use: When calling external services or unreliable dependencies

patterns.name: CQRS
patterns.description: Separates read and write operations into different models
patterns.when_to_use: When read and write workloads have very different characteristics

patterns.name: Event Sourcing
patterns.description: Stores state changes as sequence of events rather than current state
patterns.when_to_use: When audit trail and time-travel are critical requirements

patterns.name: Strangler Fig
patterns.description: Gradually migrate legacy system by routing traffic to new implementation

patterns.name: Backend for Frontend (BFF)
patterns.description: Separate backend for each frontend client type (web, mobile, etc)
patterns.when_to_use: When different clients need significantly different data shapes
patterns.when_to_avoid: When clients share nearly identical data requirements

patterns.name: Sidecar
patterns.description: Deploy auxiliary functionality alongside main service container
patterns.when_to_use: For cross-cutting concerns like logging, monitoring, proxying
patterns.when_to_avoid: When functionality can be handled by service mesh

patterns.name: Service Mesh
patterns.when_to_use: In complex microservices environments with many services

patterns.name: Saga Pattern
patterns.description: Manages distributed transactions through compensating operations

patterns.name: Database per Service
patterns.description: Each microservice owns its own database schema
patterns.when_to_use: For true service autonomy and independent scaling
patterns.when_to_avoid: When services need strong consistency across data

patterns.name: API Versioning
patterns.description: Maintain multiple API versions simultaneously for backward compatibility
patterns.when_to_use: For public APIs with external consumers
patterns.when_to_avoid: For internal APIs with controlled rollout

patterns.name: Bulkhead
patterns.description: Isolate resources to prevent failure cascades
patterns.when_to_use: To protect critical resources from less critical workloads

patterns.name: Cache-Aside
patterns.description: Application checks cache before database, populates on miss
patterns.when_to_use: For read-heavy workloads with infrequent writes
patterns.when_to_avoid: For write-heavy or real-time data requirements

patterns.name: Outbox Pattern
patterns.description: Store events in database alongside entity changes, then publish
patterns.when_to_use: To ensure atomicity between database writes and event publishing
patterns.when_to_avoid: When eventual consistency is not acceptable

patterns.name: Retry with Exponential Backoff
patterns.description: Retry failed operations with increasing delays
patterns.when_to_use: For transient failures in distributed systems
patterns.when_to_avoid: For non-idempotent operations without proper safeguards

antipatterns.name: God Service
antipatterns.description: Single service handling too many responsibilities
antipatterns.why_bad: Violates single responsibility, hard to scale and maintain
antipatterns.alternative: Split into focused microservices with clear boundaries

antipatterns.name: Distributed Monolith
antipatterns.description: Microservices that must be deployed together due to tight coupling
antipatterns.why_bad: Complexity of microservices without benefits of independence
antipatterns.alternative: Properly define service boundaries, use async communication

antipatterns.name: Chatty API
antipatterns.description: Requires many round trips to complete a single operation
antipatterns.why_bad: High latency, network overhead, poor user experience
antipatterns.alternative: Use GraphQL or composite endpoints, implement BFF

antipatterns.name: Shared Database
antipatterns.description: Multiple services directly accessing same database schema
antipatterns.alternative: Database per service, use events for data sharing

antipatterns.name: Big Bang Migration
antipatterns.description: Attempt to migrate entire system at once
antipatterns.alternative: Use Strangler Fig pattern for incremental migration

antipatterns.name: Golden Hammer
antipatterns.description: Using same technology for every problem
antipatterns.alternative: Evaluate each use case, choose appropriate tools

antipatterns.name: Anemic Domain Model
antipatterns.description: Domain objects with no business logic, just getters/setters
antipatterns.why_bad: Business logic scattered across service layer
antipatterns.alternative: Rich domain models with encapsulated behavior

antipatterns.name: Nano-services
antipatterns.description: Services that are too granular and numerous

antipatterns.name: Synchronous Chain
antipatterns.description: Long chain of synchronous service calls
antipatterns.alternative: Use async messaging or event-driven patterns

antipatterns.name: Leaky Abstraction
antipatterns.why_bad: Defeats purpose of abstraction, creates hidden dependencies
antipatterns.alternative: Design clean interfaces that hide implementation

antipatterns.name: Hard-Coded Configuration
antipatterns.why_bad: Requires rebuilds for config changes, environment issues
